_comment: Corpus manifest. Each module is benchmarked through every pipeline stage it supports; 'stages' (optional) limits which stages run. 'file' resolves to corpus/<file>; 'path' (optional, relative to bench/) references a wasm in place and is skipped when absent. 'init' (optional) names an export the host calls once after instantiate, before exec (wago has no start section, so AssemblyScript modules expose _initialize). Modules with 'exec' entries are also run end-to-end (args are i32). Tiers: synthetic micro/scale modules (isolate one codegen aspect, regenerate .wat via corpus/build.sh); 'compute' kernels (real algorithms) — the hand-written .wat set (build.sh) plus the Rust-compiled Benchmark-Game/crypto/raytracer set (corpus/rust/*.rs, regenerate via corpus/build-rust.sh, all import-free core-1.0 wasm the backend runs end-to-end); 'real'/'real-large' third-party programs (AssemblyScript libraries via corpus/build-as.sh, plus committed interpreters/engines — Lua, SQLite — and the committed multi-megabyte Ruby 3.3 interpreter (~16 MiB) and esbuild bundler (~12 MiB) — refresh all of these via corpus/fetch.sh). The real-large third-party binaries now decode, validate, AND compile on the backend (they run through the Compile/CompileFull stages) — but they still can't be instantiated/executed here because they need their host runtime (emscripten JS glue for lua/sqlite, the gojs bridge for esbuild, the rb-js-abi bridge for ruby), so they have no exec entries. All committed .wasm are checked in so the suite needs no toolchain at run time.

modules.desc: minimal module; fixed pipeline overhead
tight integer loop, no calls
recursion; call/return heavy
mixed i32/i64 arithmetic loop
f64 math loop (mul/add/sqrt/convert)
linear-memory load/store loop
recursive call tree with load/store churn at every node
mutable global get/set loop
call_indirect through a function table
br_table multiway dispatch
300 small functions; per-function compile cost
singly-linked list build + dependent-load pointer chase
escape-time Mandelbrot over an n x n grid (f64 compute)
Sieve of Eratosthenes: memory + strided marking + branches
Benchmarks-Game N-body: leapfrog solar-system integration (f64 mul/add/div/sqrt)
Benchmarks-Game spectral norm: power iteration of AtA (f64 + integer div inner loop)
Benchmarks-Game fannkuch-redux: permute + pancake-flip (branch/array churn, no f64)
dense n x n f64 matrix multiply (fp multiply-add + strided memory)
recursive in-place quicksort of n ints (recursion + branchy partition + swaps)
table-driven CRC-32 over an n-KiB buffer (integer load/xor/shift + table lookup)
standards-correct SHA-256 of an n-KiB buffer (i32 rotate/shift/xor/add, 64-round)
recursive Whitted ray tracer: n x n frame, 4 spheres + checker plane, depth-4 mirrors (heavy f64 vector math)
regex matching regression artifact; no manifest host runtime
Wasm3 interpreter provenance artifact; compile-only without its host runtime
AssemblyScript json-as (SWAR): serialize/deserialize a struct
AssemblyScript blake-as: BLAKE3 hash (SWAR) of a 4 KiB buffer
AssemblyScript utf-as: UTF-8 -> UTF-16 transcode (SWAR) of a 4 KiB buffer
Focused xjb-as open-coded unsigned 64x64 multiply-high workload
Focused utf-as byte-pack and json-as four-digit broadword kernels
AssemblyScript json-as SIMD: serialize/deserialize canonical payload
AssemblyScript blake-as SIMD: BLAKE3 hash of a 4 KiB buffer
AssemblyScript utf-as SIMD: UTF-8 decode/encode of mixed text
Lua 5.4 interpreter (emscripten build, ~270 KiB); decode+validate+compile (running needs the emscripten JS runtime — SjLj trampolines, resize_heap)
SQLite 3.46 engine (official wasm, ~920 KiB); decode+validate+compile (imports env.memory, so it needs an imported linear memory to instantiate)
Ruby 3.3 interpreter (~16 MiB, 17k funcs — the largest compile workload); decode+validate+compile (running needs the rb-js-abi host bridge)
esbuild bundler (Go -> wasm, ~12 MiB; Go runtime/GC code shape); decode+validate+compile (running needs the gojs host bridge)
